body:
“Get out,” my old man muttered, the living room stunk of his cigarettes and cheap whiskey. He didn’t look at me when he said it, just zoned out into whatever he was watching on TV, that’s all he ever seemed to do these days.

I didn’t want to stay, so there was zero point in arguing. The old man would forget anyway, although, there’s always a chance he’d yell at me later for not doing something he never actually told me to do. I walked outside to a world where the air didn’t taste like cigarettes, alcohol, and bad decisions, and took in the deep breath of the sticky humid evening air, it felt almost like breathing water, but it was still better than the air inside.

There she stood at the end of the driveway, her handsome butch silhouette leaned against the hood of a beat up ‘66 Mustang, that needed it’s share of fixin’, but could still outrace anything in this one horse town. I could still see the cherry red paint peeling and cracking at various spots, in the slowly dying summer light, but in the where rays of the sun hit it I could still imagine it as a chariot wreathed in fire. She was rarely seen wit
reblog_comment:
“Get out,” my old man muttered, the living room stunk of his cigarettes and cheap whiskey. He didn’t look at me when he said it, just zoned out into whatever he was watching on TV, that’s all he ever seemed to do these days.

I didn’t want to stay, so there was zero point in arguing. The old man would forget anyway, although, there’s always a chance he’d yell at me later for not doing something he never actually told me to do. I walked outside to a world where the air didn’t taste like cigarettes, alcohol, and bad decisions, and took in the deep breath of the sticky humid evening air, it felt almost like breathing water, but it was still better than the air inside.

There she stood at the end of the driveway, her handsome butch silhouette leaned against the hood of a beat up ‘66 Mustang, that needed it’s share of fixin’, but could still outrace anything in this one horse town. I could still see the cherry red paint peeling and cracking at various spots, in the slowly dying summer light, but in the where rays of the sun hit it I could still imagine it as a chariot wreathed in fire. She was rarely seen without grease streaked across her knuckles, from where she was tinkering with something.

She opened the passenger door, allowing me to hear the music that gave the night an almost ethereal quality. “I got half a tank of gas. Found a fifty in a jacket. And Sally is running ok tonight. Maybe this is finally Fate giving us that nudge.” Something in the way she said “Fate” something we could grab onto and it would take us the hell out of here. She bowed slightly in front of me, one hand off to the side motioning to the now open door, “Your chariot, milady.”

“You are a big dork! But I wouldn’t have it any other way!” I said with a wink. I slid into the seat, the hot cracked vinyl stinging my bare thighs, and pulled the door closed behind me. She hopped in on her side and made the engine roar to life and settle with a lovely throaty purr, that seemed to resonate with the excitement in my own chest. I grin big at her “What’s the plan?”

“I got my last paycheck today and have a little saved on top of that. So if we drive all night we can be at the overlook just in time to see sunrise and then be in the city by noon.” She looks at me, hope in her eyes. “And I should have enough for first month’s rent for the two of us.”

I nod and with that we’re off, flying down the highway as the sun sets… She slides a CD in the raido and the first song…

You got a fast car
Is it fast enough so we can fly away?
We gotta make a decision
Leave tonight or live and die this way
